name: Data
definition: Raw facts and information that can be processed.

name: Data Structure
definition: A method of organizing and storing data efficiently.

name: Linear Data Structure
definition: Data elements arranged sequentially.

name: Array
definition: A collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations.

name: One-Dimensional Array
definition: Array containing elements in a single row.

name: Two-Dimensional Array
definition: Array organized in rows and columns.

name: Linked List
definition: A linked list is a linear data structure made of nodes, where elements are stored non-contiguously in memory. Each node contains data and a pointer to the next node in the sequence.

name: Singly Linked List
definition: Singly linked list is a linked list where each node points only to the next node in the sequence.

name: Doubly Linked List
definition: A linked list where each node contains pointers to both the next and previous nodes.
real_life_examples: Music playlist next and previous song navigation
Undo and redo functionality in software

name: Circular Linked List
definition: A linked list where the last node points back to the first node, forming a circular structure.

name: Node
definition: A fundamental building block used in data structures such as linked lists, trees, and graphs, where each node stores data and one or more references to other nodes.
real_life_examples: People connected in a social network

name: Pointer
definition: A variable that stores the memory address of another variable.
math_relations: Graph connections where pointers represent edges

name: Dynamic Memory Allocation
definition: Dynamic memory allocation is the process of allocating memory for a program at runtime (while it is running), rather than at compile time. This allows for more flexible and efficient use of memory, as the program can request and release memory as needed.

cpp: int* arr = new int[5]; // Allocate memory for an array of 5 integers
cout << arr[i] << " "; // Output the array elements

real_life_examples: Managing memory for applications while they are running
Creating dynamic data structures like linked lists and trees
Operating systems allocating memory to processes
math_relations: Graph connections through dynamically allocated nodes

name: Stack
definition: A linear data structure where operations are performed only at one end, the last element added to the stack is the first to be removed (LIFO: Last In First Out).
operations: push(x): Insert x at the top
IsEmpty(): Check if stack is empty
IsFull(): Check if stack is full
Size(): Check the size of the stack

name: Queue
definition: A linear structure where the first element added is the first to be removed (FIFO: First In First Out), elements are inserted at the rear and removed from the front.
operations: IsEmpty(): Check if queue is empty
IsFull(): Check if queue is full
Size(): Check the size of the queue
real_life_examples: Store customer lines, first come, first served

name: Circular Queue
definition: A queue where the rear wraps around to the front using circular indexing, efficiently reusing empty spaces created by dequeued elements.
operations: Enqueue(x): Enqueue, insert x at rear
IsEmpty(): Check if queue is empty
IsFull(): Check if queue is full
Size(): Check the size of the queue
math_relations: cq_length_formula : length = (rear - front + maxSize) mod maxSize

name: Priority Queue
definition: A queue where elements are removed based on priority instead of insertion order.
operations: push(x, p): Inserts new element x with priority p.
top(): Access the highest priority item
math_relations: priority_order_relation : p_i > p_j => x_i served before x_j
complete_binary_tree_property : nodes filled level by level

name: Deque
definition: A double-ended queue that allows insertion and deletion at both the front and rear.
operations: push_front(x): Insert an element at the front end.
push_back(x): Insert an element at the rear end.
pop_front(): Delete an element from the front end.
pop_back(): Delete an element from the rear end.
getFront(): Retrieve (does not remove) the front element.
getRear(): Retrieve (does not remove) the rear element.
isEmpty(): Check if the deque is empty.
size(): Return the number of elements currently in the deque.

name: Recursion
definition: Recursion is a technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on smaller subproblems.

name: Recursive Function
definition: A function that calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem.

name: Base Case
definition: The stopping condition in a recursive algorithm that prevents infinite recursion.
operations: CheckCondition(): Check if the input size is minimal enough to return a value
real_life_examples: A GPS stopping its search once the destination coordinates are reached
Walking down a staircase and stopping once our foot touches the ground floor

name: Call Stack
definition: A stack used by programs to manage active function calls. Each time a function is called, it is added to the stack, executes its statements, and finally returns to where it was called from.
operations: push (Function Call): The program pushes a new frame into the stack when a function is called.
pop (Function Return): When a function finishes execution, the frame is popped off.
real_life_examples: Tracking active function calls in a program

name: Sparse Matrix
definition: A matrix in which most of the elements are zero.
operations: insert(row, column, value): Adds a non-zero element to the matrix
delete(row, column): Removes a non-zero element.
transpose(): Reverse the row and column indices of all non-zero elements.

// Implementation logic to link new node

name: Polynomial Representation
definition: A method of storing and manipulating polynomial expressions using arrays or linked lists.
operations: insert(coeff, exp): Add a new term to the polynomial
display(): Print the polynomial in a + bx^n format
add(poly1, poly2): Add two polynomials by summing coefficients of like terms
multiply(poly1, poly2): Multiply every term of poly1 by every term of poly2

name: Tree
definition: A hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges with one root node and zero or more subtrees.
math_relations: Maximum number of nodes in a binary tree with n levels: 2^n - 1
Maximum number of nodes at level i in a binary tree: 2^(i-1)

name: Binary Tree
definition: A tree in which each node has at most two children called the left child and right child.
math_relations: Maximum number of nodes in a binary tree with n levels: 2^n - 1
Maximum number of nodes at level i in a binary tree: 2^(i-1)

name: Binary Search Tree
definition: A binary tree where all values in the left subtree are smaller than the root and all values in the right subtree are larger than the root.

name: AVL Tree
definition: A self-balancing binary search tree where the difference between heights of left and right subtrees cannot be more than one for any node.
math_relations: Balance Factor = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree)
Number of edges in a tree with n nodes: n - 1

name: Heap
definition: A complete binary tree that satisfies the heap-order property.
math_relations: Height of a heap with n nodes = O(log n)

name: Max Heap
definition: A heap where each parent node is greater than or equal to its children.

name: Min Heap
definition: A heap where each parent node is less than or equal to its children.

name: Priority Queue
definition: A data structure that manages a collection of elements with priority assigned to each element.Where elements are removed according to priority.

name: Graph
definition: A collection or set of nodes(vertices) connected by edges.

name: Directed Graph
definition: A graph where edges have a direction, indicating a one-way relationship between vertices.

name: Undirected Graph
definition: A graph where edges have no direction, indicating a two-way relationship between vertices.

name: Weighted Graph
definition: A graph where edges have weights, indicating the cost or distance between vertices.

name: DFS (Depth First Search) Algorithm
definition: An algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures, starting at the root and exploring as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
code_examples: void dfsRec(vector<vector<int>> &adj, vector<bool> &visited, int s, vector<int> &res) {

name: BFS (Breadth First Search) Algorithm
definition: An algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures, starting at the root and exploring all neighbors at the present depth prior to moving to the next level(exploring nodes level-by-level).

name: Dijkstra Algorithm
definition: A greedy shortest-path algorithm for weighted graphs with non-negative weights.
code_examples: priority_queue<pair<int, int>, vector<pair<int, int>>, greater<pair<int, int>>> pq;

name: Minimum Spanning Tree
definition: A spanning tree connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight and no cycles.
code_examples: vector<pair<int, pair<int, int>>> minimumSpanningTree(int V, vector<vector<int>>& edges) {

name: Prim Algorithm
definition: A greedy MST algorithm expanding the tree using the minimum outgoing edge.

name: Kruskal Algorithm
definition: A greedy MST algorithm sorting edges by weight and adding them if they don't form a cycle/ A greedy MST algorithm selecting the smallest non-cycling edge.

name: Topological Sorting
definition: A linear ordering of vertices in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

name: Hash Table
definition: A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function(uses hash indexing).

name: Hash Function
definition: A function mapping keys to table indices.

name: Collision Resolution
definition: Methods for handling multiple keys mapped to the same index.
math_relations: collision_condition : h(k1) = h(k2), where k1 != k2

name: Sorting Algorithm
definition: Arranging elements into a specific order.

name: Bubble Sort
definition: A sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.
code_examples: for (int j = 0; j < n - i - 1; j++) {

name: Selection Sort
definition: A sorting algorithm repeatedly selecting the minimum element.
code_examples: for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
math_relations: selection_rule : select minimum element from unsorted portion

name: Insertion Sort
definition: A sorting algorithm building the sorted array one item at a time by repeatedly taking the next item and inserting it into the correct position.
math_relations: insertion_rule : insert key into sorted portion

name: Merge Sort
definition: A sorting algorithm that divides the array into smaller subarrays, sorts them, and then merges them back together.

name: STL (Standard Template Library) Components
definition: Standard Template Library components in C++ including containers, iterators, algorithms, and functors.
